citation_sentences: responses and corresponding strain hardening rates of the solutiontreated specimens were obtained from tensile tests carried out at an initial strain rate of $1 . 1 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 } { s ^ { - 1 } }$ at ambient temperature using an MTS tensile machine. Samples for impact toughness measurements were cut from the solution-treated rods with the V-notch perpendicular to the rod axis. Charpy impact energies were measured using a MTS impact tester (500 J capacity) $\mathrm { a t } - 4 0 \ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ . The geometries of the tensile and Charpy impact specimens are shown in Fig. 1. Two solution-treated specimens are tested, and the data is the average value of two specimens.

paper_title: Effect of alloying content on microstructure and mechanical properties of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels
citation_sentences: Room temperature engineering stress-strain curves of the various steels after 2 h solution-treatment at 1050 ◦C are shown in Fig. 2(a–c). It is clear that despite the similar heat treatment the engineering stressstrain curves are significantly influenced by the variations in alloy content.

As shown in Fig. 2(a), a strong increase in yield strength from 542 MPa to 1125 MPa is observed as the carbon content is increased from x = 0.6%–1.5% in a Fe30Mn11Al(x)C steel. This strength increase is, however, accompanied by a significant decrease in tensile elongation from \~60% to \~20%, reflected also in a transition from work hardening to work softening. The highest work-hardening rate was found for the steel containing a carbon content of 0.6%, while rapid work softening occurred in the steel with a carbon content of 1.5%.

In contrast, a decrease in yield strength, from 896 MPa to 401 MPa, was observed as the Mn content was increased from x = 20%–35% in a Fe(x)Mn11Al1C steel, Fig. 2(b). This decrease in yield strength was accompanied by an increase in total elongation, from 28.5% to 60%, and by an increase in work hardening rate. The variation in mechanical properties with varying Al content was similar to that for variation in C content. Specifically, the yield strength increased from 433 MPa to 846 MPa as the Al content was raised from 6% to 12% in a Fe30Mn(x)Al1C steel (Fig. 2(c)), while the elongation decreased from \~75% to \~40%.

To reveal the influence of solution temperature on mechanical properties, specimens with different Al content were also solution treated at 1100 ◦C for 2 h, again followed by water quenching. As shown in Fig. 2 (d), there is a slight decrease in yield strength with increasing solution treatment temperature comparing with that shown in Fig. 2(c), which may be attributed to an increase of grain size with increasing solution treatment temperature.

treatment at $1 0 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 2 h. In the sample with a carbon level of 0.6% (Fig. 2(e)) nano-sized κ-carbides, smaller than 1 nm in size, are precipitated in the austenite phase. The fine size of the κ-carbides, together with the influence of short-range ordering of C and Al in the austenite [4], results in very weak diffraction spots from the κ-carbides in the SAED patterns. The κ-carbides coarsen with increasing carbon content (Fig. 3(e-h)), with a corresponding increase in the intensity of the κ-carbide diffraction spots. The average diameter of the κ-carbides was measured as about 2.0 nm for the 1.0%C steel, 25 nm for the 1.2%C steel, and 80 nm for the 1.5%C steel. In each case the orientation relationship between κ-carbides and austenite (γ) was found to be (001)κ || (001)γ. A regular rectangular shape of the carbides can be clearly observed in the 1.5%C steel (Fig. 3(h)), meaning growth of κ-carbides took place with {011) as the growth plane, leading to confined growth and κ-carbide shape by the crystal orientation of the austenite matrix [22,23].

The results show that alloying by C, Mn, and Al plays a dominant role in strengthening and toughening the designed low-density steels (Fig. 2, and Table 2). With regard to the microstructure, the results show that precipitation of κ-carbides is strongly influenced by the alloying process (Figs. 3–5). It can be seen for example, as shown in Table 2, that the yield strength and impact toughness are increased from 401 MPa to 354 J for Fe1C30Mn6Al to 1125 MPa and 1.5 J for Fe1⋅5C30Mn11Al. At the same time, as shown in Table 3, the Fe1C30Mn6Al alloy does not contain κ-carbides whereas the κ-carbides reach a size of 50 nm in the Fe1⋅5C30Mn11Al alloy. Thus the mechanical properties, such as strength, ductility, and toughness strongly depend on the precipitation [24–26]. However, no detailed relationship between mechanical properties and precipitation has been reported previously in the literature.

As shown in Fig. 2, the engineering stress-strain curves are strongly influenced by the chemical composition with regard to the C, Mn, and Al content. An increase in C content significantly enhances the yield strength but is detrimental to ductility. Moreover, the Mn content can be adjusted to provide enhanced ductility but only at the expense of significantly reduced impact toughness (Table 2). With regard to reduced density the Al content is the dominant factor. However, an Al content larger than 11% results in a strongly impaired impact toughness.

paper_title: Effect of alloying content on microstructure and mechanical properties of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels
citation_sentences: EBSD maps showing phase distribution, together with grain and twin boundaries, for low-density Fe30Mn11Al(x)C steels with different carbon contents are shown in Fig. 3(a–d). Notably, for the Fe30Mn11Al0⋅6C steel both FCC austenite and BCC ferrite (about 30% by volume fraction) are present after solution treatment at 1050 ◦C for 2 h followed by water quenching (Fig. 3(a)). The average grain size of the ferrite phase is about 5.5 μm, which is slightly smaller than the average grain size of the austenite phase of 7.3 μm. For carbon contents of 1.0%C, 1.2%C, and 1.5%C (Fig. 3(b–d), respectively) only austenite is present in the microstructure, with a significantly coarser austenite grain size in each case compared to that in the 0.6%C two-phase microstructure. The smaller austenite grain size in the low-density steel with 0.6%C may be attributed to suppression of austenite grain coarsening by the ferrite phase [21]. The fractional twin boundary length, $f _ { \mathrm { T w } }$ (defined as the total twin boundary length divided by the total length of all boundaries with misorientation angle >15◦) decreases from close to 60% in the sample with 0.6%C to 50% in the sample with 1.5%C (Table 3).

The dark field images and inset selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns in Fig. 3(e–h) illustrate the size and distribution of carbides in the austenite phase for Fe30Mn11Al(x)C low-density steels alloyed with 0.6%C, 1.0%C, 1.2%C, and 1.5%C, respectively. The size of the carbides increases with carbon content, despite the solution

treatment at $1 0 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ for 2 h. In the sample with a carbon level of 0.6% (Fig. 2(e)) nano-sized κ-carbides, smaller than 1 nm in size, are precipitated in the austenite phase. The fine size of the κ-carbides, together with the influence of short-range ordering of C and Al in the austenite [4], results in very weak diffraction spots from the κ-carbides in the SAED patterns. The κ-carbides coarsen with increasing carbon content (Fig. 3(e-h)), with a corresponding increase in the intensity of the κ-carbide diffraction spots. The average diameter of the κ-carbides was measured as about 2.0 nm for the 1.0%C steel, 25 nm for the 1.2%C steel, and 80 nm for the 1.5%C steel. In each case the orientation relationship between κ-carbides and austenite (γ) was found to be (001)κ || (001)γ. A regular rectangular shape of the carbides can be clearly observed in the 1.5%C steel (Fig. 3(h)), meaning growth of κ-carbides took place with {011) as the growth plane, leading to confined growth and κ-carbide shape by the crystal orientation of the austenite matrix [22,23].

As shown in Table 3, the effects of the C, Mn, and Al alloy content on the microstructure can be summarized with regard to two aspects. The first relates to the ferrite phase volume fraction, and the second relates to precipitation of κ-carbides. As shown in Fig. 3(a–d) and Fig. 5(a–d)), the presence of the ferrite phase results in a significantly refinement in the average grain size of the austenite phase. This refinement is, however, not strongly affected by the volume fraction of ferrite phase, as evidenced by the observation that the average austenite grain size is around 7.3 μm in the 0.6%C steel (containing 30% ferrite), but only 9.9 μm in the 20%Mn steel (containing only 3.3% ferrite). In contrast, the average austenite grain size was significantly larger in ferrite-free compositions, e.g., 22.5 μm in the Fe30Mn11Al1⋅0C steel and 34.2 μm in Fe30Mn6Al1.0C. Thus, the chemical composition should be designed to ensure the presence of small amounts of ferrite phase to tune the grain size in low-density steels.

As shown in Fig. 3(e–h), Fig. 3(e,h) and Fig. 5(e–h), the κ-carbides are uniformly distributed in the austenite matrix. Their size, however, is significantly affected by the C, Mn, and Al alloying content, as shown in Fig. 6(a). In particular both the C and Al content strongly influence the

citation_sentences: The influence of Mn content on microstructure of Fe(x)Mn11Al1C low-density steels is shown in Fig. 4. Some ferrite is still present in the 20%Mn steel (Fig. 4(a)), with a ferrite grain size of about 4.1 μm and volume fraction of 3.3%. The austenite grain size and twin boundary fraction in this sample were estimated as about 9.9 μm and 62%, respectively, suggesting that even small amounts of ferrite phase can promote refinement of the austenite grain size. In the 25% Mn (Fig. 4(b)) sample the ferrite phase fraction decreases to 0.65%, with a similar ferrite grain size, estimated as about 4.5 μm, similar to that in the 20% Mn sample. The austenite grain size, however, increases to 18.8 μm. For the 30%Mn and 35%Mn samples (Fig. 4(c, d)) only a single austenite phase is present, with average grain sizes of about 23.7 μm (30%Mn) and 33 μm (35%Mn). The twin boundary fraction $( f _ { \mathrm { T w } } )$ in both samples was estimated as about 60%, indicating that this did not vary with coarsening of the grain size.

The microstructural characteristics of the κ-carbides in the Fe(x) Mn11Al1C low-density steel samples, as obtained by dark-field imaging in the TEM are shown in Fig. 4(e–h). The average κ-carbide size in the 20%Mn sample was estimated as about 18 nm, decreasing to 5 nm, 3 nm and 2 nm for Mn contents of 25%, 30% and 35%, respectively.

citation_sentences: The effects of Al content on microstructure and κ-carbides in the Fe30Mn(x)Al1C low density steels, as characterized by EBSD and TEM observations, are illustrated in Fig. 5. For Al contents of 8–11% (Fig. 5(ac)) only a single austenite phase is present, while a small amount of ferrite phase, with a volume fraction of 3.1%, was observed in the lowdensity steel alloyed with 12%Al. The average grain sizes were estimated as about 28.3 μm, 29.7 μm, and 23.7 μm for the 8%, 10% and 11% Al steels, respectively. In contrast, the austenite grain size in the 12% Al steel (Fig. 5(d)) was significantly smaller (12.6 μm). Investigation of the 6% Al steel (not shown in Fig. 5) revealed a grain size of 33.4 μm.

No κ-carbides were observed in the Fe30Mn(x)Al1C low density steel with 8% Al (Fig. 5(e)). In contrast extensive κ-carbide formation was observed for Al contents of 10% and larger (Fig. 5(f-h)). The average κ-carbide diameters were estimated as about 1 nm, 1.5 nm and 15 nm for the steels with 10%, 11% and 12% Al, respectively, highlighting a strong dependence of precipitation on the Al content, in particular at the upper end of the values explored in the present work.

As shown in Table 3, the effects of the C, Mn, and Al alloy content on the microstructure can be summarized with regard to two aspects. The first relates to the ferrite phase volume fraction, and the second relates to precipitation of κ-carbides. As shown in Fig. 3(a–d) and Fig. 5(a–d)), the presence of the ferrite phase results in a significantly refinement in the average grain size of the austenite phase. This refinement is, however, not strongly affected by the volume fraction of ferrite phase, as evidenced by the observation that the average austenite grain size is around 7.3 μm in the 0.6%C steel (containing 30% ferrite), but only 9.9 μm in the 20%Mn steel (containing only 3.3% ferrite). In contrast, the average austenite grain size was significantly larger in ferrite-free compositions, e.g., 22.5 μm in the Fe30Mn11Al1⋅0C steel and 34.2 μm in Fe30Mn6Al1.0C. Thus, the chemical composition should be designed to ensure the presence of small amounts of ferrite phase to tune the grain size in low-density steels.

As shown in Fig. 3(e–h), Fig. 3(e,h) and Fig. 5(e–h), the κ-carbides are uniformly distributed in the austenite matrix. Their size, however, is significantly affected by the C, Mn, and Al alloying content, as shown in Fig. 6(a). In particular both the C and Al content strongly influence the

citation_sentences: As shown in Fig. 3(e–h), Fig. 3(e,h) and Fig. 5(e–h), the κ-carbides are uniformly distributed in the austenite matrix. Their size, however, is significantly affected by the C, Mn, and Al alloying content, as shown in Fig. 6(a). In particular both the C and Al content strongly influence the

κ-carbide size. The increase in C content from 0.6% to 1.5% (2.5-fold) results in a sharp increase in κ-carbide size from \~1 nm to \~50 nm (50- fold). Similarly, the rise in Al content from 8% to 12% (1.5-fold) leads to an increase in κ-carbide size from 0.5 nm to 15 nm (30-fold). By comparison, only a weak effect of Mn content on the κ-carbide size is observed in Fig. 6(a), where the increment in Mn content from 25% to 35% (1.4-fold) results in a decrease of κ-carbide size just from 5 nm to 2 nm (2.5-fold). Thus, C and Al are the key elements strongly affecting the κ-carbide size.

The experimentally determined variation in density for as a function of C, Al and Mn content is shown in Fig. 6(b). For each element it can be seen that the density varies linearly with alloy content (expressed here as mass ${ \% } ,$ with density given as $g / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 3 } )$ ). A linear fit for each of the three elements suggests that the overall density can be described according to Eq. 3

paper_abstract: a Special Steel Department, Central Iron and Steel Research Institute (CISRI), Beijing, 100081, China b Laboratory of Advanced Materials (MOE), School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Tsinghua, Beijing, 100084, China c National Engineering Research Center for Equipment and Technology of Cold Strip Rolling, Yanshan University, Qinhuangdao, 066004, China
paper_title: Effect of alloying content on microstructure and mechanical properties of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels
citation_sentences: To determine the relationship between the mechanical properties and the size of κ-carbides, the strength, ductility, and impact toughness were plotted as a function of the average κ-carbide size (Fig. 7). A very good linear relationship was obtained for both the yield strength and tensile strength as a function of the square root of the size of κ-carbides. Thus, the strength of the low-density steel after solution processing can be considered as mainly being controlled by κ-carbide size, rather than by the grain size, volume fraction of ferrite, or twin boundary fraction (Table 3). The elongation to failure (Fig. 7(b)) and area reduction (Fig. 7 (c)) both linearly decrease with the square root of the κ-carbide diameter. It is suggested therefore that further design of low-density steels with both high strength and elevated ductility can be achieved by tuning the κ-carbide size, through careful adjustments in chemical composition.

Based on the linear fitting of yield strength dependence on the square root of κ-carbide size (Fig. 7(a)), a constant slope of 97.1 MPa/nm1/2 is obtained, implying a strong shear hardening from the κ-carbides. It should be pointed out that the data in Fig. 7(a) suggest that particle shear strengthening is still active for κ-carbide sizes larger as 50 nm, which is significantly larger than the typical \~5 nm critical precipitate size for the transition in strengthening from shearing mode to an Orowan mechanism [33]. The flow stress in the absence of any strengthening contribution from κ-carbides was estimated as about 478 MPa (Fig. 7 (a)), which may be explained by both solid solution hardening and austenite grain size strengthening [17,34]. The influence of the κ-carbide volume fraction still, however, requires further exploration.

Most literature reports have so far been focused on strengthening and ductility enhancement of low-density steel, while only limited efforts have been devoted to impact toughness [35,36]. The Charpy impact toughness at − ${ } ^ { - 4 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } }$ was plotted as a function of the square root of κ-carbide size, with the results shown in Fig. 7 d). The impact toughness is strongly dependent on the κ-carbide size in the solution-treated low-density steels. Low density austenitic steels can exhibit high impact toughness due to the FCC-type microstructure [37]. However, only three of the low-density steels designed in the present study possessed high impact toughness, specifically Fe30Mn6Al1⋅0C (354 J), Fe30Mn8Al1⋅0C (294 J), and Fe30Mn10Al1⋅0C (237 J) – see also Table 2. The analysis presented in Fig. 7(d) reveals that impact toughness is mainly controlled by the κ-carbide size. As such, the requirement for low-density steels with high toughness should be addressed by design of alloys with controlled κ-carbide size.

The impact of alloying content on mechanical properties can be explained by the work hardening resulting from the variation in κ-carbide size as a function of alloying content. As mentioned above, precipitation of carbides results in strengthening according to a shear strengthening mechanism. However, shear deformation also facilitates the glide of dislocations across κ-carbide shear planes, resulting in a weakened work hardening [39,40]. This explains the observed correlation between low work hardening rate and large κ-carbide size as seen in Fig. 8. As shown in Table 2, the $\sigma _ { T S }$ and $\sigma _ { F S }$ for all the examined steels were estimated at only about 1350 MPa and 2150 MPa, respectively. Thus, an increase in κ-carbide size enhances the yield strength, thereby reducing the work hardening rate and uniform elongation. Also, an increase in κ-carbide size is found to lead to a deterioration in impact toughness, as shown in Fig. 7(d), thereby resulting in a decreasing non-uniform ductility (or area reduction). One remaining unexplained issue, however, is the observation of very similar values of $\sigma _ { T S }$ and FRS for low density steels with and without κ-carbides, as this cannot be interpreted by variations in κ-carbide size. The origin of the nearly unchanging values of $\sigma _ { T S }$ and $\sigma _ { F S }$ may be related to the unique microstructural evolution, and should be studied further in future work.

caption: Fig. 8. Work hardening behavior of low-density steels alloyed with different C, Mn, and Al content followed by solution heat treatment at the temperature, in which the solid lines and the dashed lines show the true tensile stress-strain (SS) curves and the work hardening (WH) rate curves, respectively: (a) effect of $\mathrm { c }$ content after solution treatment at 1050 ${ } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ (b) effect of Mn content after solution treatment at $1 0 5 0 \ { } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ (c) effect of Al content after solution treatment at 1050 ◦C, (d) effect of Al content after solution treatment at 1100 ◦C.

paper_title: Effect of alloying content on microstructure and mechanical properties of Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels
citation_sentences: To gain a better understanding of the effects of alloying content, the true stress-strain curves and corresponding work hardening rates were calculated. The results are plotted in Fig. 8. The $\sigma _ { T S }$ was estimated as about 1350 MPa for all the designed low-density steels in the present study. Thus, microcracks will nucleate at this strength, and the $\sigma _ { T S }$ is not significantly affected by the $\mathrm { C } ,$ Mn, and Al alloying content. Similarly, the $\sigma _ { F S }$ was estimated for all the investigated steels as about 2150 MPa (Table 2), implying that fracture strength also is not affected by the alloying content. Furthermore, this implies that both the $\sigma _ { T S }$ and $\sigma _ { F S }$ do not vary with microstructure, as the ferrite phase fraction and austenite grain size vary significantly over the investigated range of low-density steel compositions. As seen in Fig. 8(a), the work hardening rate increases with decreasing carbon content, with a maximum work hardening rate obtained in the 0.6%C steel (around 1900 MPa). Increasing the Mn content results in an increase of the work hardening rate, up to a maximum work hardening rate of around 2300 MPa in the 35%Mn steel (Fig. 8(b)). Similar to the effects of carbon content on the work hardening rate, the work hardening rate increases with decreasing Al content, with a maximum work hardening rate of around 2100 MPa found for the 6%Al steel, as shown in Fig. 8(c). As discussed above the $\sigma _ { F S }$ and σ are do not change significantly with variation in $\mathrm { C } ,$ Mn and Al content after solid solution treatment at $1 0 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ and a similar result is also found for the samples solution treated at $1 1 0 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (see Fig. 8(d)). Note that high ductility is only generated in low density steels with a high work hardening rate capacity [38,39]. It is worth noting also that except for the steels with 0.6C and 30Mn content, the variation of work hardening rate with true strain presents a typical appearance, with a rapid decline followed by a slow rise and then another decline [4,24], while the steels with 0.6C and 30Mn content show a monotonic decrease of work hardening rate with increasing true strain.

The impact of alloying content on mechanical properties can be explained by the work hardening resulting from the variation in κ-carbide size as a function of alloying content. As mentioned above, precipitation of carbides results in strengthening according to a shear strengthening mechanism. However, shear deformation also facilitates the glide of dislocations across κ-carbide shear planes, resulting in a weakened work hardening [39,40]. This explains the observed correlation between low work hardening rate and large κ-carbide size as seen in Fig. 8. As shown in Table 2, the $\sigma _ { T S }$ and $\sigma _ { F S }$ for all the examined steels were estimated at only about 1350 MPa and 2150 MPa, respectively. Thus, an increase in κ-carbide size enhances the yield strength, thereby reducing the work hardening rate and uniform elongation. Also, an increase in κ-carbide size is found to lead to a deterioration in impact toughness, as shown in Fig. 7(d), thereby resulting in a decreasing non-uniform ductility (or area reduction). One remaining unexplained issue, however, is the observation of very similar values of $\sigma _ { T S }$ and FRS for low density steels with and without κ-carbides, as this cannot be interpreted by variations in κ-carbide size. The origin of the nearly unchanging values of $\sigma _ { T S }$ and $\sigma _ { F S }$ may be related to the unique microstructural evolution, and should be studied further in future work.
